C:
Here's the funnel, then where to put your effort first.

## Where to focus first

**The onboarding → first completed workout segment — and before you touch it, nail down what your activation event actually is.** That's the answer for ~90% of fitness apps, and the reasoning is mechanical: activation gates everything downstream. A 10% lift in the share of new users who reach first value multiplies through every later conversion step, so it's worth more than the same 10% lift at the paywall. It's also the leakiest part of most fitness funnels and the cheapest to fix (friction reduction, not pricing strategy). Teams reflexively tune the paywall because that's where money visibly changes hands — that's the trap. The money is decided upstream.

Two caveats that could move the answer: if your data shows onboarding is already tight (>60% reach first workout) but week-2 retention collapses, focus shifts to the habit loop instead. And the "focus first" only holds once you've defined activation correctly — optimizing toward the wrong event makes the whole funnel worse.

## The funnel

I'd model it as seven stages. The first four are activation; the last three are monetization.

| # | Stage | Event | Typical leak | Lever |
|---|-------|-------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Acquire | Install → first open | 10–20% never open | Store listing match, fast first launch |
| 2 | Set up | Goal + profile + (account?) | 30–50% | Cut steps, defer account creation, show progress |
| 3 | First value started | First workout/session begun | 20–30% | One obvious "start here" CTA, no choice paralysis |
| 4 | **Activated** | **First workout completed** | 15–25% | Short first session, can't-fail difficulty, instant payoff |
| 5 | Habit | 3 sessions in 7 days / D7 return | 40–60% | Reminders, streaks, scheduled plan, day-2 re-engagement |
| 6 | Intent | Paywall reached → trial start | varies | Paywall timing + framing |
| 7 | Paid | Trial → paid conversion | 40–70% of trials | Value before charge, trial reminders |

## Define the activation event before optimizing

Activation is the single early behavior most correlated with eventual paid retention — not a guess, a finding. Pull your cohort data and ask: which first-week action best separates users who are still paying at week 4 from those who churned? For fitness it's usually a *count-in-window* metric, not a single action — e.g. **"3 completed workouts in the first 7 days."** One workout proves the app runs; three proves a habit is forming, and habit is what people pay to keep. Use that as your North Star and design stages 2–5 to drive toward it.

## The one strategic fork: paywall placement

This is the decision that needs your judgment, not a default:

- **Hard paywall after onboarding (before first workout):** maximizes trial starts, but you're asking for a card from someone who hasn't felt value — higher trial volume, lower trial→paid quality, more refunds/cancels.
- **Delayed/soft paywall after first value (or after the habit signal):** fewer trials, but each one comes from someone who's already experienced the product — higher conversion quality and retention.

If activation is your weak point (the likely case), a hard paywall actively hides the value that would make people pay. Lean toward delaying the paywall until at least the first completed workout, and A/B test placement against the *paid-retention* metric, not trial-start rate — trial starts will mislead you here.

## Instrument it so the focus decision is data-driven

Track each transition as its own event with timestamps so you can measure not just conversion but **time-to-activation** (median hours from install to first completed workout — shorter is strongly predictive). Segment every stage by acquisition source; a paywall that converts paid social traffic can crater on organic, and a blended funnel hides that. Once stages 1–5 are firing, your first build target is whichever step has the largest `drop-off % × downstream value` — which, before you have the data, I'd bet is stage 2→4.

If you share your real stage-by-stage numbers (or your PostHog funnel), I can pinpoint the actual leak and order the fixes instead of working from benchmarks.

Here's the funnel, then where to put your effort first.

## Where to focus first

**The single biggest bottleneck is stage 2 — Set up (goal + profile + account), which loses 30–50% of users, the largest drop of any stage in the funnel. Fix that step first — and before you touch it, nail down what your activation event actually is.** That's the answer for ~90% of fitness apps, and the reasoning is mechanical: setup sits at the top of the activation band, so it gates everything downstream. Every user it sheds is a user who never reaches first value, never builds a habit, and never sees the paywall. A 10% lift in the share of new users who clear setup multiplies through every later conversion step, so it's worth more than the same 10% lift at the paywall. It's also the cheapest part to fix (friction reduction — cut steps, defer account creation, show progress — not pricing strategy). Teams reflexively tune the paywall because that's where money visibly changes hands — that's the trap. The money is decided upstream, and the biggest single hole is setup.

Two caveats that could move the answer: if your data shows setup is already tight (say 2→4 conversion is healthy and >60% reach first workout) but week-2 retention collapses, the biggest leak moves to the habit loop (stage 5) and your focus shifts there instead. And the "focus first" only holds once you've defined activation correctly — optimizing toward the wrong event makes the whole funnel worse.

## The funnel

I'd model it as seven stages. The first four are activation; the last three are monetization.

| # | Stage | Event | Typical leak | Lever |
|---|-------|-------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Acquire | Install → first open | 10–20% never open | Store listing match, fast first launch |
| 2 | **Set up (biggest leak)** | Goal + profile + (account?) | **30–50%** | Cut steps, defer account creation, show progress |
| 3 | First value started | First workout/session begun | 20–30% | One obvious "start here" CTA, no choice paralysis |
| 4 | **Activated** | **First workout completed** | 15–25% | Short first session, can't-fail difficulty, instant payoff |
| 5 | Habit | 3 sessions in 7 days / D7 return | 40–60% | Reminders, streaks, scheduled plan, day-2 re-engagement |
| 6 | Intent | Paywall reached → trial start | varies | Paywall timing + framing |
| 7 | Paid | Trial → paid conversion | 40–70% of trials | Value before charge, trial reminders |

Within the activation band (stages 1–4), stage 2 is the worst single leak, which is why it's the place to start. Stage 5 (Habit) loses a comparable share but sits in the retention band — address it second, once setup is tight.

Activation is the single early behavior most correlated with eventual paid retention — not a guess, a finding. Pull your cohort data and ask: which first-week action best separates users who are still paying at week 4 from those who churned? For fitness it's usually a *count-in-window* metric, not a single action — e.g. **"3 completed workouts in the first 7 days."** One workout proves the app runs; three proves a habit is forming, and habit is what people pay to keep. This is the decision that needs your judgment, not a default:

- **Hard paywall after onboarding (before first workout):** maximizes trial starts, but you're asking for a card from someone who hasn't felt value — higher trial volume, lower trial→paid quality, more refunds/cancels.
- **Delayed/soft paywall after first value (or after the habit signal):** fewer trials, but each one comes from someone who's already experienced the product — higher conversion quality and retention.

If activation is your weak point (the likely case), a hard paywall actively hides the value that would make people pay. Lean toward delaying the paywall until at least the first completed workout, and A/B test placement against the *paid-retention* metric, not trial-start rate — trial starts will mislead you here.

## Instrument it so the focus decision is data-driven

Track each transition as its own event with timestamps so you can measure not just conversion but **time-to-activation** (median hours from install to first completed workout — shorter is strongly predictive). Segment every stage by acquisition source; a paywall that converts paid social traffic can crater on organic, and a blended funnel hides that. Once stages 1–5 are firing, your first build target is whichever step has the largest `drop-off % × downstream value` — which, before you have the data, I'd bet is stage 2, the setup step (highest drop-off, and it sits above the entire rest of the funnel).

If you share your real stage-by-stage numbers (or your PostHog funnel), I can confirm whether setup is in fact your biggest leak and order the fixes instead of working from benchmarks.
P:
**Bottom line:** Focus first on the activation span — signup to a formed habit — and within it the **setup → first-completed-workout** drop. That's where most fitness apps leak the most users, and the fixes are friction reduction (cheap) rather than pricing strategy (expensive and risky). Two conditions on that answer: define your activation *metric* from your own cohort data before optimizing toward it, and don't let the visible-money reflex pull you to the paywall first — the paywall mostly *reflects* decisions made upstream.

## The funnel

Seven stages. Stages 1–5 are activation; 6–7 are monetization. The two stages that matter most to name correctly are **first value** (stage 4 — the "aha") and the **activation metric** (stage 5 — the count-in-window that statistically predicts who keeps paying). The percentages below are benchmarks to replace with your real numbers, not claims about your app.

| # | Stage | Trigger event | Typical drop here | Primary lever |
|---|-------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Acquire | Install → first open | 10–20% never open | Store listing matches the ad; fast, no-login first launch |
| 2 | Set up | Goal + profile (account?) | 30–50% | Cut steps; defer account creation until after value; show a progress bar |
| 3 | Start value | First workout/session begun | 20–30% | One unmistakable "start here" CTA; remove choice paralysis |
| 4 | **First value (aha)** | **First workout completed** | 15–25% | Short, can't-fail first session; instant visible payoff |
| 5 | **Activated** | **3 completed workouts in 7 days** | 40–60% | Reminders, streaks, a scheduled plan, day-2 re-engagement |
| 6 | Intent | Paywall reached → trial start | varies | Paywall timing and framing |
| 7 | Paid | Trial → paid | 30–60% don't convert | Deliver value before the charge; trial-ending reminders |

## Why this step first

The argument is mechanical, not a preference. Activation gates everything downstream, so a 10% lift in the share of users who reach first value flows through every later step — it's worth more than the same 10% lift at the paywall, where it only acts on the few who got that far. Stage 2→4 is also usually the **leakiest** span in a fitness funnel and the **cheapest** to fix: you're removing friction, not re-architecting pricing. Prioritize by `drop-off % × downstream value`; before you have the data, that product almost always peaks at the setup-to-first-workout span.

Two things that would move this answer:
- **If setup is already tight** (say >60% reach a first completed workout) **but week-2 retention collapses**, your bottleneck is the habit loop (stage 4→5), not onboarding. Shift there.
- **The "focus first" only holds once activation is defined correctly.** Optimizing toward the wrong event makes the whole funnel worse — you'd be pushing more people toward something that doesn't predict payment.

## Define the activation metric before optimizing

Activation isn't a guess; it's the earliest behavior most correlated with eventual paid retention — a finding you pull from cohort data. The question to ask: *which first-week action best separates users still paying at week 4 from those who churned?* For fitness it's almost always a **count-in-window** metric, not a single tap. One completed workout proves the app works; **three in seven days** proves a habit is forming — and the habit is what people pay to keep. Make that your North Star and design stages 2–5 to drive toward it. (First completed workout stays in the funnel as the *aha* gate — it's necessary but not sufficient.)

## The one real judgment call: paywall placement

This needs your decision, not a default:

- **Hard paywall after onboarding, before first workout** — maximizes *trial starts*, but you're asking for a card before anyone has felt value. Expect lower trial→paid quality and more refunds/cancellations.
- **Delayed/soft paywall after first value (or after the 3-in-7 habit signal)** — fewer trials, but each comes from someone who's already experienced the product, so conversion quality and retention are higher.

If activation is your weak point — the likely case — a hard paywall actively hides the value that would make people pay. Lean toward delaying the paywall to at least after the first completed workout, and **A/B test placement against paid retention at day 30, never against trial-start rate.** Trial starts will point you the wrong way here: the hard paywall "wins" on that metric while losing on revenue.

## Instrument it so the call is data-driven

- **Fire each transition as its own timestamped event**, so you measure not just conversion but **time-to-activation** — median hours from install to first completed workout. Shorter is strongly predictive; watch it as a leading indicator.
- **Segment every stage by acquisition source.** A paywall that converts paid-social traffic can crater on organic, and a blended funnel hides it.
- Once stages 1–5 are firing reliably, **build against the single step with the largest `drop-off % × downstream value`.**

Give me your real stage-by-stage numbers (or read access to the PostHog funnel) and I'll locate the actual leak and order the fixes by expected revenue impact, instead of working from these benchmarks.
